text: The patient was a thirty-two-year-old female at the twentieth week of pregnancy. She presented with abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting and constipation. She had a previous abortion and she had no previous surgery. Abdominal ultrasonography revealed dilation of ileum without any inflammation around appendix. Abdominal MRI was performed for further evaluation and revealed diffuse dilation of jejunum and ileum consistent with ileus.
She had six days of bowel rest and conservative medical treatment without any response. Later on, laparoscopic exploration was planned.

The patient is positioned supine following general anesthesia and endotracheal intubation. The surgeon inserts the first trocar in the left upper quadrant using Hassan's technique, and the peritoneal space is insufflated. A second 5mm trocar is inserted under direct laparoscopic vision.

The laparoscopic exploration reveals an enlarged uterus occupying the pelvis and lower abdomen. Elongated loose bands attached to the distal edge of the omentum above the uterus are identified and divided. Additional five-millimeter trocars are inserted to enhance maneuverability. The search for Treitz's ligament is hindered by the enlarged uterus. Fragile and edematous bowel loops are observed, and a bowel grasper is used to carefully handle the intestine, preserving its integrity and preventing iatrogenic injury.
The surgeon identifies the trite's ligament and continues the exploration towards the ileocecal valve, searching for any pathological changes along the bowel.

The laparoscopic view shows the surgeon ceasing exploration due to the incarceration of a segment of the small intestine. The camera angle is adjusted to explore the distal parts, revealing the pathology. The intestines are re-evaluated from the terminal ileum up to the lesion, with no additional pathology observed.

Congenital bands located approximately ten centimeters proximal to the ileocecal valve are observed strangulating the related intestinal segment. These bands originate from the right lateral abdominal wall and extend towards the cecum. The surgeon divides the bands using ligature, successfully reducing the internal herniation and freeing the intestinal passage.
The surgeon assesses the blood circulation to the jejunal segment, confirming it is sufficient, and observes no additional intra-abdominal pathology during the laparoscopic exploration.
The surgeon removes the trochars under direct vision, completing the surgery without complications. The surgical field is clear, and the instruments are withdrawn smoothly.
